speaker: interviewer
text: --six. I'm conducting an interview with Vera Gissing, and this is tape number four. You-- you just said why you have moved to Wales.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- actually, before we moved to Wales, I-- I recall very vividly one night in March, 1943. We older ones were allowed to listen to 9 o'clock news. And I remember being there with my friends and listening. And it was on that occasion when the man who spoke on the radio announced clearly and precisely what was happening to Jews in the camps. Until that day, I hoped that my parents were still in the staging post-- that garrison town of Terezin, or Theresienstadt, as it's popularly called-- where I felt they may be living comparatively normal life-- at least that came over from the news which we got. But of course, after hearing about the deprivation and the horrors of the camps, one could never be sure where they were. And life for us, who left their parents behind, was never the same after that night. We have to live with this new fear. And it was a terrible fear. And once again, I found it invaluable to have the support of the children and the teachers around me. And I cried for my sis, who was so alone in a completely English environment-- of people who might-- who cared for her, but could not understand no more than the Rainfords could or the Campions could. You know, they sort of felt-- even my sister felt that such atrocities, as we were hearing, surely could not have gone on. I mean, one human being could not do this to another human being. And I think many people felt like this. But back in our-- when we-- when we moved to our Welsh haven, and we had this beautiful hotel with a boating lake and a bit of the local river running through the grounds, and-- and a lovely new teacher, Miss Mackenzie, who was a Scottish lady, supplied, of course, by courtesy of the British Council, who introduced us to hockey and selected me as the captain. You know, life went on. And-- and life had to go on. And you had to come to terms with living a happy life of a teenager and being at the same time consumed with fear and almost guilt because you were able to live this happy life. And the time went on so quickly and so pleasantly, holidays spent with my foster family or with my sister, and always the term time to look forward to. Because it was-- it was like a home to me then. But as time went and 1945 came-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --then one began to be very nervous and very impatient. You know, once the British forces invaded France and started marching across Europe, we waited so impatiently for any news. And I wrote, and wrote, and wrote in my diary. And it was so difficult to concentrate on studies and on any other things. And then the news finally came. I was the first child of the school to hear that mother had survived. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My sister had a telegram from friends in Stockholm who wrote to say that both mother and father had survived-- congratulations-- and a letter following. And everybody in the school was so happy for me, so thrilled for me. And I couldn't believe my luck. It was the only prayer I ever prayed, the only wish I ever had. But it was short lived. Because when the little note came from Stockholm, which was written in my mother's handwriting, and it was sent from Bergen-Belsen, my father's signature wasn't on it. It was my mother's and my auntie's signature, Auntie Berta-- her sister's signature. So then, of course, I worried about father. And I worried because there was no more news of mother. But then something else arrived, these little printed cards from Red Cross-- from the Red Cross, where they ticked, I'm alive, you know. I'm well. I'm returning to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was I with all this proof. And yet, I wasn't happy-- not just for my father. I had that niggling premonition. And I kept saying to myself why, why I have it? And then the lists came of those who survived Belsen. And my mother's name was there, and my auntie's name was there. And even though I knew they must have suffered the most horrific time there, I-- at least I had it again in proof that they were alive, that mother had survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I enlisted in the first transport back to Prague. I was due to leave in August, 1945. And shortly before I left, I was already in London. No, I wasn't-- sorry. I was with Auntie Marjorie-- Mrs. Campion-- saying goodbye. And I had a letter from my sister. And she always wrote such a scrawl. You know, it was difficult to decipher. And the letter was immaculately written. And straightaway, I knew there was something more wrong. And-- [SIGHS] --she broke the news to me so gently-- so thoughtfully. To put it in a nutshell, my mother had survived Bergen-Belsen. She saw the British army marching. And she lived to see the end of the war. But she died two days later of typhoid. And I think that was the cruelest blow God had ever dealt me and my sister. Because one was prepared for the worst, but we were given back our mother. And yet before we could even meet, she was taken away without seeing us. And the very next day, we had a letter from a completely unknown person to say that as far as they knew, our father had been shot on a death march at the end of 1944. [SIGHS] Nevertheless, I was determined to go back. First of all, by then, I knew my aunt, mother's sister, had survived and was there, and that she had been with my mother most of those awful years. And I wanted to learn everything I could about their fate and fate of the other members of our family. Also, I mean, I was brought up in the Czech school. We were brought up to go back to our homeland to help to rebuild it. There was no thought of me staying. And though both the Rainfords and Auntie Marjorie, even my bossy Auntie Marjorie, wanted to adopt me and wanted me to stay, there was no other thought in my head than to go back. Eva couldn't go back at the same time, because she was taking her finals in a few months time, her nursing finals, and-- finals, and it seemed sensible in view of the fact that our parents hadn't survived that she should do those before contemplating a return. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in a special plane. It was a war plane adopted for taking kids back again. Were there many children who traveled with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were about 14 others in the plane. And you know, each one of us were orphaned. Out of all those children in the school, and there were hundreds of them who came on the transport, there was only one father who survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, we went, I think, in a Lancaster bomber. And I remember flying over Cologne and seeing the devastation of the city, and then flying over Prague. It was such a bright, sunny August day. And Prague was, of course, almost untouched by bombs, which was wonderful. But the homecoming was so strange. There was no one waiting for me, because no one knew when the transports were bringing in the children. But actually, my friend, Alice, whom I mentioned earlier, who's my best friend, her oldest sister who came with the liberating army to-- to-- to Theresienstadt, when that was freed, she had been going every single day to the airport in case Alice had appeared. And so, of course, she let my aunt know I had arrived. And she came for me the next day. But those of us, you know, who had no parents to go to, they had a very hard time of it. Because the government made them stay in hostels-- the ones who were sort of my age. And I was 16-- so I think from 15 onwards-- in hostels, where people who'd come back to Prague, from the concentration camps, and had nowhere to go were living. Now, these people, of course, were in dreadful states-- mentally, emotionally, physically-- as you can imagine. And you can imagine what a terrible atmosphere it was for a child coming into-- when he'd just been orphaned, and just returned, and to be subjected to that. And he didn't know-- they didn't know how to cope. And in those days, there was no counseling, no help given. [SIGHS] I'll come to that later, because there's an interesting epitaph to that. My aunt, when she came for me, I could hardly believe what I was seeing. And I was overwhelmed with a feel of warmth and love for her. She'd always been skinny. Everyone predicted she would never return. But she was the sole survivor. But she stood there, and she really was skin and bones. But it was her eyes. They were just like pools of incredible, deep sadness. I-- I just wanted to hug her. And I wanted to love her. And I wanted to say I'll be always there for her. And I did just that. And I think she felt the same. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It must have been very strange, because she said goodbye to me. I was such a young, [LAUGHS] little kid with whom she had been exasperated so many times. And yet suddenly, I was the strong one. I learned from her, of course, that in that camp, she didn't just bury my mother. But she also buried my two cousins, Tommy and Janus. And all three of them died after the liberation-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --one on the 1st of May, one on the 21st of May, on his 16th birthday, and my mother on the 10th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they her children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, this was that uncle. You know, she was a spinster, if you remember. She was the spinster who lived with my grandparents. Thomas-- Tomas' and Janus' parents died, we think, in Lodz. I think, you know, they were gassed. Because the professional families from Prague, most of them went-- went to Lodz straight without going to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] I still got Auntie Marta's engagement ring. And this is my mother's. Anyway, my mother-- my auntie-- as I said, Auntie Berta, lived with her parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a beautiful flat. But after they'd been taken to the camps, the flat was given to the local butcher, who had a young family. And no way could she get the flat back. They were loading it with-- with her father's furniture, you know, and everything. And she had to make do in sharing a two room flat which belonged to a cousin of my mother's and his wife. Their name was Karel and her name was Milla, who were quite a bit younger. And of course, it was the younger ones who survived more easily, because they were useful as work-- workhorses, and stronger. And so I moved in there as well. I didn't mind the cramped conditions. And luckily, they didn't. We all needed each other. We helped each other, though nobody could talk about the past at that time. Nobody could. But I did learn that Karel, who's the same namesake of my father, was together with my father in the camps almost to the end. And Milla was together with Aunt Berta and my mother. So that created a tremendous bond, of course, between us. And there were lots of heartwarming things said, which we did talk about-- my mother's courtship with my father when Karel, who was a young lad then-- was their go between, and you know, one who brought love letters from one to the other. And he said how he fell in love with my mother and hoped that she wouldn't marry that Karel. Because he wanted to grow up and marry her. And how my mother, because she was a marvelous cook, when they were in Hamburg, she was cooking actually for the Germans-- German staff there. And she always managed to sneak some food. And she was keeping my aunt, and my Aunt Milla, and other people alive by helping them to survive by adding, you know, to their diet; and of course, risking her own life by pinching the food; and things like that. I started going to school as soon as I arrived. Luckily, for me, there were two of my classmates from England in the same gymnasium. I said luckily, because it was so strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, in our school, in Wales, we were treated with kindness, and with compassion, and thoughtfulness. And here, back in Czechoslovakia, although everybody knew we were orphaned and the way our parents had died, and the rest of our families, they almost resented the fact that we had survived. And you know, they sort of looked upon us as if to say, what are you doing there? I thought Hitler got you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were impregnated with this antisemitic doctrine which had been dished out by the Germans during those long years of occupation. Because it did pass after, you know, after a couple of years. I would say that Czechoslovakia, particularly the Czech parts and the Moravian parts, are the least antisemitic in Europe. But obviously, there was some-- some there. And they were also envious of the fact that we spent the war years [LAUGHS] in England, forgetting that we had all the bombing. And we also had shortages; and you know, apart from us, as I said, being separated from our parents. So it was this that was very difficult to contend with. One of the masters-- because most of Czechoslovakia was liberated-- this was by agreement between Churchill and-- and the-- and the Americans and Stalin-- that the Russians would liberate Czechoslovakia. Because of this, of course, the-- the Russians were in vogue. And they exploited this fact. And Russian had to be taught in all the schools, and had been since Prague was liberated, which was in the spring of last year. And of course, I knew no Russian. And I had a merciless Russian teacher who punished me and derided me at every turn, because I found it difficult to catch up quickly with-- with the language; and sneered at me in such a manner. When I was trying to make a life with my aunt, who was a [SIGHS] chronic depressant, for whom life became a burden because she survived, and filled with guilt because she buried so many; and as a 16, 17-year-old, you know, I had to cope with that. It was a very difficult. And then coping with the antagonism I got at school made it impossible. And one day, I came back. And by then, actually, we'd moved out. I managed to get the housing office to let us have one room in what used to be my father's flat. It was the room-- the tiniest backroom, sunless room, in which our maid used to live. And we had that in the tiny little scullery where there was a tap-- cold water tap. We weren't allowed to use the bathroom. We were allowed to use the toilet of what was her flat. You know, it was all so unjust. But at least we had a little bit which was our own. And as Auntie Milla was expecting her first baby, we knew knew a two-roomed flat couldn't house five of us. But-- so we were already there. And the only facility for cooking was one little burner, gas burner. And then there was a little steel-- well, it wasn't steel. It was copper. No. It was iron or something-- little gas oven, portable oven. And I just turned it on and put my head in it. I wanted to commit suicide. I just felt I couldn't cope. But luckily, the only girlfriend I made in the class felt that I was really, really upset. I-- I left. I haven't said anything to anybody. But I just sort of rushed out of school, out of class. And she came after me and stopped me being successful. And I was very glad, very glad I wasn't. But it was so hard, you know, to come to terms with that sort of treatment. And then when I went for the first time to my hometown, that again was-- it took an awful lot of courage to walk down the streets which formed my, you know, my childhood, towards the house in which we lived. Nobody knew I had come, and nobody recognized me immediately. And I went-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to the staircase which led to our large flat. And you know, before I left, I wrote. It was a silly thing to do, but probably a natural thing to do. I wrote in my childish hand on the wall of the lavatory, goodbye, my lavatory. And father, in one of his many letters, said that they were having the whole flat decorated. But he decided they weren't going to touch the lavatory so that we could alter that saying together when I'd came back. And anyway, I rang the bell of our flat. And of course, a strange woman answered. And I went inside. She just beckoned me in. She-- I didn't have to say anything to her. And I first went on the veranda, enclosed veranda, where my father used to have his afternoon naps and I used to play with Marta. And there was a strange young lad on-- on the floor mending his bicycle. And the toilet in the bathroom ran off the veranda, and I impulsively opened the door to the lavatory. And there it was still-- goodbye, my lavatory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just cried inwardly. I'm here, father, but where are you? Why aren't you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just ran away from there, and I ran out. And I ran through the rest of the town to the banks of the river Elbe where we so often played. And I buried my face in the-- in the grass. And for the first time since my return, I cried and I cried all those bitter tears which needed to come and were so hard to come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I dried my tears and sat in-- deep in thought, and turned back towards the town, I saw a young girl coming towards me. She was running. She had pigtails. And as she saw me, she smiled, and I recognized her. [LAUGHS] She was my bosom friend Marta. Somebody must have recognized her, or maybe the people where I-- you know, who lived in our house had sent the message. But there she came. And of course, she hugged me. And we cried and laughed together. And with her support, you know, the pains eased. I didn't bring auntie with me purposely, because I didn't want her to see me suffering. She suffered. She had enough to suffer without that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I stayed with her that night and telephoned auntie and asked her, could I stay the night? She was coming the next day, because my parents had put some of our most precious possession with different people. And you know-- and she knew exactly who had what, because my mother obviously had told her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and Marta and I, we slept in the same bed, and we talked through the night. And her-- she had lost her sister in the war, but that was through natural causes. And her mother made me my favorite meal which my mother used to make such splendidly-- which were dumplings, but fruit dumplings, you know, filled with fruit and covered in lashings of curd cheese, and runny butter, and sugar, and all the things one shouldn't eat today. But-- so it was a sort of a bittersweet visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next day, when auntie came, we went around the people who were given my mother's spare coats, and our bound books, and things like that. And do you know? Mother's best friend denied she'd ever had anything. Yet she had a big extension built [LAUGHS] to her house with my parents' money. And months later, I saw her walking the streets in my mother's fur coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some of the people who were either our servants who worked for us, or worked in the office, or who were just, you know, ordinary poor folks, they gave everything, and would have given much, much more would we-- if we had-- if I had taken it. So sorry they were at loss-- at the loss of such wonderful people as my parents were. But my visit there made me aware that I didn't belong anymore. Yet, my heart belonged and would always belong there. And some days later, another young lady, but much older than me, well in her 20s, appeared at auntie's flat-- my auntie's flat-- auntie's room. She had been working for my mother as a secretary in the office. Her name was Manya or Maria, I called her. And she was courting at the time she worked for mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --was very fond of Maria. But her own mother was opposed to her fiance. But Maria desperately wanted to get married. And the mother said, you won't have any dowry, and he won't have you. And she took off the full wage of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother, unknown to the-- to Maria's mother, started assembling a whole dowry for her. She gave her a raise-- unofficial raise-- which-- of which the mother was not informed. And she started buying her sheets, and pillowcases, and you know, all the-- well, all the normal things one buys for a dowry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so anyway, Maria told me this. And she said, I loved your mother so very much. And remember Vera, she said to me, you were a bridesmaid at my wedding. And I sure was. I've got a lovely photograph of me as a bridesmaid at her wedding. And she said, now it's my turn to do something for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to stop you. But it's the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. That went quick.
